---
title: Local env file
description: Create, edit, inspect, and view one encrypted env payload.
---

This guide is the core **better-age** loop before Varlock or team sharing enter the picture.

## Create the payload

```sh
bage create .env.enc
```

The file is visible in your project, but its contents are encrypted:

```txt
# better-age encrypted env payload
# Docs: https://github.com/PaulSenon/better-age
# This file is safe to commit only if your policy allows encrypted secrets.
# Do not edit the armored block manually.

-----BEGIN BETTER AGE PAYLOAD-----
-----BEGIN AGE ENCRYPTED FILE-----
...
-----END AGE ENCRYPTED FILE-----
-----END BETTER AGE PAYLOAD-----
```

## Edit secrets

```sh
bage edit .env.enc
```

**better-age** decrypts the payload, opens your editor, validates the env text when the editor closes, then writes the encrypted payload back.

Example env text:

```txt
DATABASE_URL=postgres://localhost:5432/app
API_TOKEN=dev-token
```

If the env text is invalid, **better-age** lets you reopen the editor with your edited content preserved.

<Callout type="info">
  `edit` temporarily writes plaintext to a private temp file while your editor is open. **better-age** cleans it up afterward, but editor backups, swap files, crash recovery, and plugins are outside its control.
</Callout>

## Inspect without secrets

```sh
bage inspect .env.enc
```

Use `inspect` when you want metadata, env key names, recipients, and compatibility state without opening secret values.

## View as a human

```sh
bage view .env.enc
```

`view` opens an in-process terminal viewer. It is for humans reading the env file.

It does not print plaintext to stdout.

## Load as a machine

```sh
bage load --protocol-version=1 .env.enc
```

`load` prints raw env text to stdout after prompting for your passphrase. This is the machine boundary used by Varlock.

For normal app runs, prefer [Varlock runtime](/docs/guides/varlock-runtime).
